questions.question: How do you start creating AI Meeting Notes in the Notion desktop app?

options.text: Click the 'New Meeting' button in the sidebar
options.explanation: While the sidebar has a 'New' option for the list view, the primary way to start a new block is by using the slash command.

options.text: Type /meet into any page in the desktop app
options.explanation: According to the article, you type '/meet' into any page in the desktop app to create AI Meeting Notes.

options.text: Select 'Start Transcribing' from the Home page
options.explanation: The Home page is used to access the feature from upcoming events, but the slash command is the standard method to create a block.

options.text: Connect your calendar first, then it happens automatically
options.explanation: While connecting calendar is helpful, you can type '/meet' to manually create a block before or without connecting.

questions.question: What is a key limitation of using AI Meeting Notes in a web browser?

options.text: It cannot transcribe audio at all
options.explanation: The browser can transcribe audio, but it has specific limitations regarding which inputs it can capture.

options.text: It only captures mic inputs and cannot capture system audio
options.explanation: The article states that in the browser, only mic inputs will be transcribed and system audio won't be captured.

options.text: It requires a specific browser extension to function
options.explanation: The feature works directly in the Notion browser app without requiring third-party extensions.

options.text: It cannot connect to external conferencing tools like Zoom
options.explanation: While it captures audio from the mic, it specifically notes issues with capturing audio from conferencing tools if using headphones.

questions.question: Which companies are listed as subprocessors for processing audio in AI Meeting Notes?

options.text: Google, Microsoft, and Zoom
options.explanation: The article lists specific subprocessors, none of which are Google, Microsoft, or Zoom.

options.text: OpenAI, Anthropic, Fireworks, and Baseten Labs
options.explanation: The article explicitly states these four companies are the subprocessors used to record, process, and generate insights.

options.text: AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud
options.explanation: While cloud providers are used generally, the specific subprocessors named are the AI/ML providers listed in the text.

options.text: Salesforce, HubSpot, and Slack
options.explanation: These are collaboration tools, not the specific AI providers listed as subprocessors for audio processing.

questions.question: Regarding legal considerations, what is the recommended best practice for obtaining consent?

options.text: Obtain consent from only one participant if they are in the US
options.explanation: Laws vary by location and industry. The recommendation is to comply with the strictest laws.

options.text: We recommend complying with the strictest consent laws and always obtaining consent from all participants
options.explanation: The article states that determining which consent laws apply can be complicated, so the strictest approach is recommended.

options.text: Consent is not required if the meeting is internal to the company
options.explanation: Legal requirements for recording/transcribing communications apply regardless of whether the meeting is internal or external.

options.text: You only need to ask consent if recording video, not audio
options.explanation: The consent requirements apply to both recording and transcribing communications.

questions.question: How does the 'Meetings' tab list view differ from the 'Default meetings database' setting?

options.text: The list view is a database where you can store all notes, while the setting determines where new pages go
options.explanation: The list view is a consolidated view of notes regardless of where they are stored, whereas the default database is specifically for storage locations.

options.text: The list view shows only notes from the default database, and the setting controls privacy
options.explanation: The list view is distinct from the default database setting; it aggregates notes you created plus linked notes from calendar events.

options.text: The list view is a consolidated view of meeting notes you created and linked to calendar events, regardless of storage location, while the default database is where new pages are stored
options.explanation: The article explains that the sidebar list view shows a consolidated view of all notes, whereas the default database setting determines where new pages are created.

options.text: They are the same feature; the list view is just a different name for the database
options.explanation: The list view is a sidebar management tool, while the default database is a settings configuration for storage locations.

questions.question: How long is audio retained on the mobile app if processing fails?

options.text: Permanently
options.explanation: The audio is not kept forever; it is retained only for a specific duration to allow for retry.

options.text: Up to three days
options.explanation: The article states that if processing fails, the file is retained for up to three days to reattempt processing.

options.text: Until the user manually deletes it
options.explanation: Even manual deletion is limited by the processing failure retention window.

options.text: It is deleted immediately upon failure
options.explanation: The system keeps the file for a short period to attempt processing again.

questions.question: Can AI Meeting Notes identify and label specific speakers?

options.text: Yes, it identifies them by name automatically
options.explanation: The article specifically states that speakers are not identified or labeled.

options.text: No, speakers are not identified or labeled
options.explanation: The text says 'speakers won’t be identified or labeled' when AI Meeting Notes detects a change in speaker.

options.text: Yes, if you add their names to the agenda beforehand
options.explanation: Agenda items help the AI structure the summary, but they do not identify speakers in the transcript.

options.text: Only on Enterprise plans
options.explanation: This feature is not available on any plan.

questions.question: How can you automatically share meeting notes with calendar event participants?

options.text: By toggling on 'Auto-share with internal calendar event participants' in Settings
options.explanation: The article states you can toggle this on in Settings → Notion AI.

options.text: By setting the page permissions to 'Anyone with link'
options.explanation: This refers to manual sharing, not the automatic feature.

options.text: By connecting the Notion Calendar app
options.explanation: While connecting the calendar is necessary to trigger the event, the auto-share setting is a separate toggle in Notion AI settings.

options.text: By default, it is always shared with all workspace members
options.explanation: By default, notes are private to the creator; auto-sharing must be manually enabled.

questions.question: What is the minimum requirement for AI Meeting Notes to generate a summary?

options.text: At least 10 minutes of meeting time
options.explanation: The requirement is much shorter, roughly one minute of spoken content.

options.text: At least 300 transcribed characters (roughly one minute)
options.explanation: The article specifies that at least 300 transcribed characters are required to generate a summary.

options.text: At least 5 minutes of recording
options.explanation: This is a longer duration than required by the feature.

options.text: No minimum time is required
options.explanation: A minimum duration of roughly one minute is required to generate a summary.

questions.question: What is the default privacy setting for custom summary instructions?

options.text: Public to the workspace
options.explanation: Custom instructions are not public by default.

options.text: Private by default — only you can see and use them
options.explanation: The article explicitly states that custom summary instructions are private by default.

options.text: Shared with anyone who has edit access to the page
options.explanation: Even if others have access to the meeting block, they see their own default instructions, not your custom ones.

options.text: Deleted immediately after the meeting summary is generated
options.explanation: The instructions remain in the list and can be used for future meetings.

questions.question: How does Notion determine if a user is joining a meeting to send notifications?

options.text: By scanning the screen for calendar icons
options.explanation: Notion does not scan the screen for icons.

options.text: By observing if a process on the computer is actively using the microphone
options.explanation: The desktop app observes if a user has a process running that is actively using their microphone (e.g., Zoom).

options.text: By connecting to the calendar API in real-time
options.explanation: It does not rely on calendar API events for the initial detection notification; it relies on system process monitoring.

options.text: By listening to the audio coming from the microphone
options.explanation: Crucially, the article states Notion does not listen to the audio for this purpose.

questions.question: Where does the consent message play from during a meeting?

options.text: Through the computer speakers
options.explanation: The article specifies that the consent message plays through your computer speakers.

options.text: Through the meeting conferencing tool (e.g., Zoom)
options.explanation: It plays through the computer speakers so everyone in the room can hear it, not through the conferencing tool.

options.text: Through the user's headphones
options.explanation: You should disconnect headphones so the message can be heard by everyone in the room.

options.text: It is displayed as a text notification on the screen
options.explanation: While text is an option, the 'Voice' option specifically plays the message through speakers.

questions.question: How many recent meeting audio files are stored on the recorder's local device?

options.text: Unlimited
options.explanation: There is a specific limit on how many files are stored locally.

options.text: 10 most recently recorded meeting audio files
options.explanation: The article states that 'The 10 most recently recorded meeting audio files will be stored'.

options.text: Only the most recent file
options.explanation: More than just the most recent file is retained locally.

options.text: None by default, unless manually selected
options.explanation: Even though local storage is off by default, if enabled, it stores the 10 most recent files.

questions.question: How are permissions handled for the transcription, notes, and summary from an AI Meeting Notes block?

options.text: They are private to the workspace owner
options.explanation: Permissions are inherited from the page, not the workspace owner.

options.text: They inherit the permissions of the page they are saved on
options.explanation: The article states that 'Meeting Notes inherit the permissions of the page they're saved on'.

options.text: They are shared with all meeting participants automatically
options.explanation: This is only true if the specific auto-sharing setting is enabled; otherwise, access is strictly page-based.

options.text: They are public by default for easy collaboration
options.explanation: By default, notes are private to the creator unless shared manually.

questions.question: Who can delete locally stored audio files?

options.text: Any member of the workspace
options.explanation: Access to local audio is restricted to the person who initiated the recording.

options.text: Only the recorder
options.explanation: The article notes that 'Only the recorder has the ability to download a locally saved audio file'.

options.text: Only if the workspace owner enables 'Store audio locally'
options.explanation: Even if enabled, only the recorder can delete the files.

options.text: Administrators in the company settings
options.explanation: The permissions are tied to the specific user action (recording), not general admin rights.
